sentences.text: By train, passenger train, to Ohrdruf.
That was in November 1944.
In Ohrdruf there-- we were put in in a camp where there were soldiers before, German soldiers, like stationed, whatever it was.
And the conditions was not so bad in the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There-- with-- was established a camp, a part of Buchenwald.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were-- not very-- 18 kilometers from Ohrdruf was a little town called Arnstadt.
And there, the Germans, they start to build in the mountains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They [INAUDIBLE], but it was the beginning.
And we had to work there 24 hours in shift.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to me personally, I think that was the worst camp I ever been.
In Ohrdruf.
I call this Ohrdruf because Arnstadt was the place where Ohrdruf we did work 24 hours.
So that way, I call this Ordruf the camp.
But there, on the place Arnstadt people that fall like-- like flies, everyday.
I remember I work there one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was glad to come back to the camp.
Somehow, some way, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became a cook there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not only a cook, but I was one of the top.
That I got orders there for the-- how many people to cook.
Everyday I could see, I have for less and less.
People are not be able to come home.
They die.
As plain as this.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that was not bad for me personally, and my brother.
And also, a lot of people.
Whomever I could, I could help, which-- even being out now here in Toronto, I have some friends.
We've been together.
They never forget me.
But nevertheless, a lot of people didn't survive there.
The whole camp, I think, was about 5,000 people.
But everyday, they come from other camps, and then go what people die.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- when I was the cook, I worked with some-- with some Russian people, Ukraine, and Polish people.
We had a few Jewish people there, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one time there come another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] head of the camp.
His name was Stibitz-- a very bad man.
And then another chief in the cook-- in the kitchen.
And they threw it out, all the Jewish people from the kitchen.
And I was one of them, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we were very good to each other in the kitchen.
When they threw me out, then some other people helped me.
But nevertheless, I had to go to work.
When I went-- so they put me in in a commando where-- outside, I would say a kilometer or a kilometer and a half from the camp.
When you went out from the camp, you turn to the left, and go as far-- there was a-- a little forest.
And then you turn again to the left.
And-- and about 50 yards from there was dig a grave.
I would say almost as big as a hockey arena.
And the deep was about-- I would say about 15 feet.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had to work there not for long-- I think it was for about a week.
What they brought in that people, and we had to sort them out in the grave and put them like sardines.
The head between the legs.
And sometime it was so bad, you have to take a dead person-- and [SIGHS] nobody from us was used to it.
But nevertheless, we had to do the-- the work.
So they told us, the SS, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how to do it.
So we-- they said, take it off, a branch from the tree, what it did go like this, and hook it on here.
And drag it down, and so put it down.
And this was a terrible thing to do.
Very terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
When I came home, my brother didn't go.
My-- when they took me to that work, my brother had to go to another work into Ohrdruf, because it was bombed, to clean it up.
So I didn't see him, and I wrote a letter.
And I said, this is God's will.
This is the way.
I don't think so I will see you again.
Happens, he did come home-- in the barrack.
And I did come in-- in the barrack.
And it was a-- a moment I've never forget.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I think it was about eight days, I finished that work.
And because I was so good, understanding with some other people, being in the-- they try, and they give me another job in a block.
So I was there until it was liquidation of the camp.
And they-- later on, when they liquidated, they supposed to send us, I know, to Bergen-Belsen-- no, to Buchenwald.
And from Buchenwald to Auschwitz somewhere.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we marched-- we're supposed to March until Buchenwald and stay there over for a while.
But in the meantime at night, they come, and we slept-- they said, go over here in Arnstadt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And overnight.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were rooms there.
And they put it in, 40, 50 in a room.
I think now our room was about 40 people or 35.
And we stayed there until about-- I would say about 5:00 or 5:30 in the morning.
It was cold, and it was dark.
They knocked on the door, the SS.
[GERMAN]-- get out, get out.
And we simply-- we did sit on the door.
Simple as this.
We didn't know what we are doing.
But say, what can-- what could happen to us?
The worst thing could happen-- they could kill us.
Or we're going to be killed tomorrow anyways.
And somehow, someway, they went away, and we were left there.
After an hour later, we looked out-- nobody's there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was already in Arnstadt.
So we didn't know, but we could hear bullets are coming, and we didn't know from where.
So we went out, and the mountain was a veranda.
And we were tailors.
And my brother had a-- so, you know, a-- that uniform.
And they always had put it in in the back, cut it out a piece, and put it in a piece of different color, just in case we escape.
So somebody will know we are from camp, we did escape.
And he cut it off, the whole part, the top.
And took it out, that piece.
So-- and I had a-- a brand-new [NON-ENGLISH] made.
Because I was in the kitchen, everybody wants to do some work for me.
And they made a beautiful [NON-ENGLISH]--
The uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was so beautiful made.
Tailored, you know, by hand, everything.
The man is here, who made it for me.
Yeah, Belzyki.
He's from our town.
He still-- he lives on [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on, we went down from the mountain.
And we went then in a-- where the civil workers did work.
And we did stay there five days.
On the sixth day, two SS with a motorcycle, they did come by.
And one with a machine gun came in into the room and ask us, or we did see-- there were seven people.
Seven people from Ohrdruf.
We did see some Americans?
We said no, in German.
And I remember one guy from [INAUDIBLE], he said to him, maybe he's got some cigarettes in German.
He spoke-- and I was surprised.
And he took out some cigarettes and left there for him.
And we were ready to attack him when he did come over.
But it did turn out all right.
And then they went that way.
And the next day, we said, we have to go.
We went out and marched about-- I would say about an hour.
And we met Americans.
We didn't know that they are Americans.
We met Americans with tanks.
And I had some bread from camp.
It was like a brick.
And I remember, I give to that Americans as a souvenir, that bread.
And he looked at over, and he gave me toast bread.
We couldn't even eat that toast bread.
We said, what kind of bread is this?
It was like chewing gum.
But nevertheless, that was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was our moment.
We jump on the tank.
Later on, some of the Americans, they call the German, like, village-- what they call-- Burgermeister-- I will say, like, the mayor.
And he said to provide us with where to stay and where to sleep.
So he took us to his farmhouse.
And he said to us, we're going to sleep with the cows together.
That was very good.
We were happy.
At least we had some straw, and we slept, and then this.
And that's all.
We could have go easily in and threw them out and go then, but we didn't do it.
We did behave very good.
And there were chickens there on the farm.
I remember my brother.
He went out and took some eggs, raw eggs.
And said Dave, I have some eggs- raw eggs.
He did eat.
And later on, two-- two days, or a day later- we went bank-- back to Ohrdruf from there to the camp where we used-- there were a lot of people there already.
Free.
And then the President Eisenhower came.
If ever-- maybe you saw a picture.
Yeah.
I stayed in the back.
You can hardly see me.
But you could see in the front one-- what was a cook, too, from Austria.
Feldman.
His name was Fel-- is Feldman.
He was playing in the Vienna Hakoah, if you ever heard about Vienna Hakoah.
That was the best team, the soccer team-- of world, very best.
And then was one guy Otto, too.
He also worked.
He was also from Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in that time, they were, laying around, people.
And he did looked over, Eisenhower, like this.
And I remember I, in that time, I had a paper.
And I didn't know what's about.
I should have kept that paper.
The newspaper, with the picture.
And then from Ohrdruf, was about 45 kilometers, was the name of the city, Gotha.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went to Gotha.
We went to the City Hall.
They provide us with, this, and with clothes and eat, and where to live.
But we couldn't sleep.
They provide us a room so beautiful, everything.
We slept on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then somehow, somewhere, one American came over.
I think there was from the CIC.
They said to me, I couldn't speak a word English.
But one was a Jewish guy.
He said, you're going to go in in that store, and you're going to watch [INAUDIBLE] people are Germans coming in and changing.
That was a department store.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in that time, the-- the owner from the store, his name was Feldman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He came over, and he said, this car is going to be for you.
You will use the car.
And I didn't know how to drive, not a bit.
I did drive at home, I did drive a wheelbarrow, not a car.
[LAUGHS]
So he's drive it-- show it to me, how to drive.
He did drove.
He right away got connection with Americans.
And he drive a beautiful BMW with a driver, with a chauffeur.
The chauffeur show it to me, how to drive.
And I drove.
No purpose.
I was so excited.
Never had a car in my life.
I drive!
He gave it to me, because I-- the Americans put me in-- in the store.
They said a manager, I knew about manager.
I saw a car.
I didn't need a store.
I didn't need nothing.
After?
I can't tell you exactly.
That was March-- April, I think.
April-- would be the 1st-- between the 1st and the 5th.
But
Because the 7th of April-- was it the 7th?
I was free.
Or was it the 7th of May?
I know-- I know the war was still on.
The 6th of May exactly.
I was free already from the Americans.
With the Americans.
Only a few days.
Only a few days, about six days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's right.
I was free.
I remember being in Gotha already.
Some airplanes, German airplanes, did [INAUDIBLE].
And then I could-- we could see the American-- some airplane took off, and they shut them down.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] From that time, I start to try to trace where is my sister.
Chuma, what she is in Toronto.
In the camps.
Because I knew she was in Auschwitz with me, and then later on-- Then she was liberated in Theresienstadt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She knew before that I am alive with my brother, that I knew from her.
Because people were-- they were transferred to Buchenwald, all the same guy what is on the picture.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To Buchenwald.
From Buchenwald, they went to Theresienstadt.
They supposed to go to Vienna.
And he met my sis-- he said, oh, your brother's there.
For sure, they are free.
That way, she knew.
And then wherever we went, in every camp, there was a registration, Jewish people, mostly.
They're registered-- from where you are, or you have some family in Canada, or in the States.
To give their name and everything.
We marked it on, and right away, that was transferred from one camp to the other.
And it was put in in the newspaper in the States.
And my sister did see a paper like this in Theresienstadt.
She knew already.
From the offices, what they-- it was very good organized.
As a matter of fact, my uncle-- I had an uncle from before the war in Los Angeles.
And when they are registered, we said, we have an uncle in Los Angeles, and his name is Willy Jacobs.
And he knew also.
She-- from Theresienstadt, Czechoslovakia, she went to Poland, Tomaszow.
She thought, where everybody's going to be alive, we're going to meet in Tomaszow.
But we didn't know anything about.
One man came over.
His daughter was liberated in Bergen-Belsen.
And he got to know in Tomaszow that she is in Bergen-Belsen.
And he came over from Tomaszow to Bergen-Belsen.
After the war, already.
And then he told us my sister is at Tomaszow.
So my brother went to bring her to Bergen-Belsen.
And I didn't see him, so I went over there, too.
And that time was the pogrom in Kielce.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, let's go back.
And we took her, we went back.
Bergen-Belsen.
And right away.
In Bergen-Belsen when-- why-- I was not liberated in Bergen-Belsen.
What was the reason I came?
When we were in Gotha, when I did look for my sister, I went all over Germany to look for her.
And I came over to Bergen-Belsen-- she was not there.
A lot of people from Tomaszow.
A lot of girls, they were there.
And my sister wasn't there.
And there were one girl-- she could speak English.
And she did work for the-- for the-- the English commandant-- like the-- the head office in Bergen-Belsen.
No, no, no.
They were already transferred.
Not that-- not that dead camp.
No, the displaced persons camp, after the war.
Right.
Right, right.
And she did work for.
And she took me in, me and my friend there.
And this is Jacobs, he came from [INAUDIBLE].
He said, [INAUDIBLE], and he show-- took out a paper.
And he said, in two weeks, the Russians-- they're going to take over Thuringer.
This include [INAUDIBLE] of Gotha Buchenwald.
Oh, we got scared.
So after two days, we went back there.
We took my brother.
We took all the belongings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And went to Bergen-Belsen with the car.
Were you given any special duties or responsibilities at the displaced persons camp, at the DP camp at Belsen?
No, not that time, when I came with the car.
Later on, there was a-- a guy, his name was Josele Rosensaft.
Maybe you heard about him.
He was the president of the British zone.
And from the camps.
He was a real guy.
I had a car.
He didn't know anything about.
He said Dave, let's make a-- a committee.
I said, why are you bothering me?
Who knows a committee.
I have a car, I need a committee?
And he became a very, very big man.
Josele Rosensaft.
And so how does he figure into the events that you're describing?
What did he do?
He had a big mouth.
He's a-- he was a little guy.
Your mo-- your mother would know him.
She will tell-- Josele Rosensaft.
She would know.
No, I didn't work.
That was not for me.
Then later on, I joined, because I had the car, and I joined the American Joint Distribution Company.
They didn't need my car, but they are registered.
Their name my car.
Oh I-- we worked there, we brought food from Bremen, where it was shipped from the States, from Canada, from all over.
It was shipped to Bremen.
We brought food.
There was the head office in Bremen.
And for pay Pesach there'd come matzah.
And I ran all over Germany to give matzah.
And then later on, we transported people to Sweden through Denmark.
We left them off Denmark.
We brought people from Berlin, Jewish people, in order to send them also to Sweden.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything by car, by truck, by ambulance.
And I drove a lot.
Yeah.
We got the most information being in Blizyn.
In Blizyn.
In that time, we knew.
The same thing what it had happened to my parents.
Treblinka.
Perished.
Nojech was with me, all the time.
Yeah, we survived together, too.
Naftula?
Naftula, no.
We went also to Treblinka.
And Berju?
Berju was killed before.
He went to Lublin.
Before, they took some people, young people, to Lublin to work.
And he died.
Approximately what year was he taken?
They took a lot of young people there.
No.
He passed away.
In-- [SIGHS] apparently, he died.
So my cousin told me from San Francisco that he was there, and he knew that he died.
